n.name: n
tips: Draw n before almost everything else. Its stem weight, arch shape, and counter proportions are the DNA for h, m, u, and r — and indirectly for nearly every other lowercase letter.
Start with a rectangle for stem width.
Draw arch as separate overlapping shape.
Counter affects readability at text sizes.
Duplicate first stem for second leg.

o.name: o
tips: The lowercase o is the curve master. Its bowl, stress axis, and stroke weights are reused in c, e, b, d, p, q, and g. Get o right before drawing any of those.
Requires overshoot at top and bottom.
Stress axis must be consistent across curves.
Do not just scale down uppercase O.
Bowl weight = n crossbar weight in low contrast.

h.name: h
tips: h = n with the left stem extended to the ascender line. Everything below x-height is identical to n.
Arch begins at same height as n.
Serif at top should match l and b ascenders.
Ascender height must match l exactly.

m.name: m
tips: m = n doubled. Copy arch and second leg from n, duplicate it rightward. Three stems must be identical weight.
Counters should feel optically equal to n counter.
Widen letter if counters feel pinched.
Tighter side bearings than n.

u.name: u
tips: u is not a mirror of n. The open top catches more light, making u appear wider. Narrow the bowl ~5-8 units for balance.
Bottom curve needs overshoot like o.
Exit stroke top-right matches n arch exit.
Transition from stem to curve must be smooth.

l.name: l
tips: l is your pure stem. Width, weight, and terminal treatment define DNA for i, j, b, d, h, k, p.
Height sets ascender benchmark.
Foot/serif treatment must match all ascending letters.
Side bearings are reference for uprights.

i.name: i
tips: i = l stem + tittle (dot). Tittle size requires careful optical calibration.
Tittle larger than feels right — check at small sizes.
Tittle shape should match period/punctuation.
Position between x-height and ascender line.

j.name: j
tips: j = i + descending hook. Hook style should match f, r, and c terminal vocabulary.
Reach descender line with overshoot.
Hook style matches your curved terminals.
Narrow side bearings like i.

b.name: b
tips: b = h stem + o bowl. Not a mirror of d. Bowl attaches at x-height and baseline.
Narrower bowl than standalone o.
Smooth junction at attachment points.
Baseline overshoot like o.

d.name: d
tips: d = o bowl + ascender stem. Source for single-story a.
Ascender height matches l and h.
Smooth transition bowl-to-ascender.
Baseline overshoot matches o.

p.name: p
tips: p = b bowl + descending stem. Stem descends below baseline.
Descender length matches g, j, q, y.
Confirm ascender-to-descender ratio.
Stem starts at x-height top-left.

q.name: q
tips: q = o bowl + descending stem. Source for single-story g.
Tail matches j and g descender style.
Transition bowl-to-stem should be smooth.
Same descender depth as p.

c.name: c
tips: c = o opened on right. Terminal flows into e, f, r.
Aperture width is a stylistic signature.
Top and bottom terminals balanced.
Same overshoot as o.

e.name: e
tips: e = c + crossbar. Crossbar slightly below vertical center.
Lower crossbar = optical stability.
Upper bowl smaller than lower bowl.
Top terminal matches c exactly.

s.name: s
tips: Copy c, mirror bottom. Spine carries same visual weight as thick strokes.
Use two o guides for optical center.
Spine angle steep = modern; flat = soft.
Top lobe smaller than bottom lobe.

a.name: a
tips: Mirror s vertically for body. Use d bowl and q stem.
Overhang (roof) is difficult — defined by angle/curve.
Bowl matches d and q shapes.
Ear echoes r arm or c terminal vocabulary.

z.name: z
tips: Establish diagonal angle. Thick stroke is the diagonal.
Diagonal thickening is severe; measure it.
Terminals match C and S style.
Balance above and below counters.

v.name: v
tips: Sets diagonal angle for w, y, x, k. Heavy left, thin right.
Tapering creates extra visual space in bearings.
Apex style (sharp/flat) is key indicator.
Heavy = downstroke (left).

H.name: H
tips: Primary capital foundation. Uprights and crossbar define DNA for 75% of caps.
Crossbar slightly above optical center.
Crossbar lighter than uprights.
Side bearing is reference for upright capitals.

O.name: O
tips: Secondary foundation letter. All cap curves derive from O (C, G, Q, D).
Essential overshoot top and bottom.
Wider than H — classical proportion.
Stress axis match lowercase o.

A.name: A
tips: A = inverted V + crossbar. Apex treatment and crossbar position are key design decisions.
Left diagonal is thick (downstroke), right is thin (upstroke).
Crossbar slightly below center.
Apex style determines tone.

S.name: S
tips: Uppercase S follows lowercase s logic at cap height. Top lobe slightly smaller than bottom.
Iterate multiple times.
Reference C/O for DNA.
Spine must match heavy weights.

OPT-001.name: Horizontal Bar Weight
OPT-001.description: Horizontal strokes appear heavier than verticals of identical width due to how the eye scans text. Horizontal bars must be thinned to look equal.

OPT-002.name: Overshoot Missing on Round Glyphs
OPT-002.description: Round and pointed glyphs must extend slightly beyond the baseline and x-height to appear optically aligned with flat-topped/bottomed glyphs.

OPT-003.name: Diagonal Stroke Compensation
OPT-003.description: Diagonal strokes appear heavier than verticals of the same mathematical width and require thinning.

OPT-004.name: Crossing Diagonal Alignment
OPT-004.description: When two diagonals cross, they appear misaligned unless offset. A geometric center crossing looks wrong.

OPT-005.name: Overshoot Inconsistency
OPT-005.description: All round glyphs in a font should share a consistent overshoot value. Mixed overshoot values create visual inconsistency.

phases.name: Lowercase Foundation
phases.description: Build n, o, H, O first - they define the DNA

phases.name: Uppercase
phases.description: Capitals derive from H and O

commonTerms.overshoot: Small extension beyond baseline/cap-height for optical balance
commonTerms.counter: Enclosed or partially enclosed space in a letter
commonTerms.bowl: Curved stroke that encloses a counter
commonTerms.ascender: Part of lowercase letter extending above x-height
commonTerms.descender: Part of lowercase letter extending below baseline
commonTerms.terminal: End of a stroke without a serif
commonTerms.serif: Small stroke at the end of a main stroke
commonTerms.x-height: Height of lowercase letters without ascenders/descenders
commonTerms.baseline: Line on which most letters sit
commonTerms.sidebearing: Space between glyph outline and advance width edges
commonTerms.aperture: Opening of a partially enclosed letter
